there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality  
$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$  
where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $. the fractional part of a real number is the difference between the number and its integer part, and it always lies in the interval $ [0, 1) $. the inequality involves the sum of the fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, ..., up to $ x^{99} $, and it is compared to $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $. this is a complex inequality that requires careful analysis of the behavior of the fractional parts of powers of $ x $. the key challenge is to find a real number $ x > 1 $ that is not an integer and satisfies this inequality. the fractional part of a number is always less than 1, so each term in the sum is less than 1, and the sum of these terms is less than 100. since $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $ is extremely small, the inequality is highly restrictive. the fractional parts of $ x^k $ for even $ k $ are particularly important because they are not affected by the integer part of $ x $. the sum of the fractional parts of $ x^k $ for odd $ k $ is also significant, but it is still bounded by 100. the inequality is designed to test the understanding of fractional parts and their behavior under exponentiation. the solution requires finding a value of $ x $ such that the sum of the fractional parts of its odd powers is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $. the fractional part of $ x $ is denoted by $ \{x\} $, and it is crucial to analyze the behavior of $ \{x^k\} $ for various values of $ k $. the inequality is highly sensitive to the value of $ x $, and any deviation from the expected behavior can cause the sum to exceed the threshold. the fractional parts of $ x^k $ for even $ k $ are not affected by the integer part of $ x $, which makes them more predictable
